core_intuitions: The RL loop is five phases (rollout -> reward -> experience prep -> minibatch updates -> weight sync), and each phase is a DIFFERENT kind of system glued at a seam: generation is a memory-bound autoregressive inference workload, training is a compute-bound gradient workload.

Generation dominates wall-clock (typically 60-80%) DESPITE having fewer FLOPs than the backward pass. The reason is arithmetic intensity: autoregressive decode must stream all N weights from HBM to emit just ONE token per sequence, so it is memory-bandwidth-bound and runs at single-digit MFU, while training reuses each weight across the whole batch and runs compute-bound at 40-55% MFU. Optimize the rollout, not the gradient.

On-policy vs off-policy is not binary but a continuous 'staleness' dial s = how many optimizer steps old the sampler weights were. The PPO/GRPO importance-ratio clip exists precisely so we can tolerate small BOUNDED staleness, which is what lets us reuse expensive rollouts for several updates instead of regenerating after every single gradient step.

Overlapping generation and training is the single biggest throughput lever. The ladder runs synchronous-serial (each machinery idles the other) -> disaggregated -> pipelined one-step-stale (train on batch k while generating k+1) -> fully async; production lives at bounded staleness so the clip stays valid while the expensive generators never idle.

The subtlest correctness bug in the whole loop is numerics consistency: the sampler engine and the trainer compute log-probs with different kernels/dtype, so the importance ratio drifts from 1.0 even at step 0 when the policy has not moved. The fix is to recompute the 'old' log-probs on the TRAINER so numerator and denominator share numerics; the sampler's log-probs become diagnostics only.
Everything must be masked to response tokens and aligned to next-token targets (shift by one); the per-response advantage is broadcast to its tokens.

existing.name: gtloop-staleness-dial
existing.issue: Clean horizontal gradient dial from s=0 (on-policy) to s->inf (off-policy) with three annotated stops and a shaded 'where real LLM-RL lives' band. Reads correctly in both themes; the gradient bar, tick markers, cards, and band label are all legible. Metaphor (a dial, not a switch) exactly matches the chapter's framing.
existing.improvement: Minor only: the 'strictly on-policy' card's second line runs close to the card edges; a hair more horizontal padding would tidy it. Not blocking.

existing.name: gtloop-five-phases
existing.issue: Vertical five-phase flow with per-phase owner pills (inference engine / verifier / trainer / FSDP / theta->generator), edge labels for the data handed between phases, and a dashed wrap-around arrow closing the loop. Correctly foregrounds Phase 1 as dominant and Phase 4 as fastest. Animation (traveling dots) is enhancement; the static frame is fully understandable. Works in both themes.
existing.improvement: None needed. The owner pills touch the box top-right corners slightly but stay legible.

existing.name: gtloop-phase-cost-ranking
existing.issue: Horizontal stacked bar (generation ~70%, exp-prep ~15%, bwd ~10%, reward/sync few %) plus a ranked legend and a 'why generation dominates despite FEWER FLOPs' inset contrasting 6N compute-bound vs 2N memory-BW-bound. This is the chapter's central quantitative fact and the figure lands it. Both themes read well.
existing.improvement: In dark mode the exp-prep segment's dark label sits on a semi-transparent accent fill with slightly low contrast; nudging that label to a higher-contrast ink would help. Non-blocking.

existing.name: gtloop-async-ladder-timelines

Cleanly conveys the overlap win and the exact one-step-staleness cost. Legible in both themes; the reduced-motion fallback keeps the static frame complete.
existing.improvement: The tiny 'weights k-1' label overlaps its arrow a touch; could be nudged for polish. Not blocking.

new_figures.name: gtloop-logprob-numerics-consistency
new_figures.concept: Why the importance ratio drifts from 1.0 at step 0 (sampler vs trainer numerics differ), and the fix: recompute 'old' log-probs on the trainer so numerator and denominator share numerics.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter calls this 'the single subtlest correctness issue in the whole loop' and 'a favorite source of silent RL bugs,' devoting a full warning box and a dedicated diagnostic ('ratio at the first minibatch of epoch 0'). No existing figure touches it. The intuition is counterintuitive and highly visual: the two log-probs come from the SAME weights yet disagree because the arithmetic pipelines differ, so the ratio -- which should be exactly 1 when the policy hasn't moved -- silently drifts and the clip engages on phantom off-policyness. A before/after diagram makes the fix ('share the pipeline, not just the policy') click instantly.
new_figures.placement: Recomputing log-probs (the "old" forward) and the reference
new_figures.brief:
A two-panel 'bug vs fix' infographic built around ONE shared token stream and a ratio readout. Top of figure: a single row of identical token tiles (o_1 o_2 ... o_t ...), labeled 'the SAME response, the SAME policy weights theta'. Establish up front that nothing about the policy differs -- only the arithmetic pipeline that scores it.

PANEL A -- 'The bug (trusting the sampler's logprobs)'. Draw two distinct compute pipelines feeding that token stream: (1) SAMPLER pipeline -- a box labeled 'inference engine (vLLM/SGLang)' with small sub-tags 'its attention kernel / maybe fp8 or quantized path / its reduction order', emitting log pi_behavior (the ratio DENOMINATOR); (2) TRAINER pipeline -- a box labeled 'trainer (FSDP, full bf16)' with sub-tags 'different attention kernel / different dtype path', emitting log pi_theta (the ratio NUMERATOR). Show the ratio r = exp(new_lp_trainer - old_lp_sampler) feeding a horizontal 'ratio track' with a reference line at 1.0. Because numerator and denominator come from DIFFERENT pipelines, draw the ratio as a wobbly line that departs from 1.0 and drifts wider as token position t grows (compounding over the sequence) -- keep it schematic, no numeric axis, just a '1.0' reference line and qualitative wobble/drift, plus a small clip band [1-eps, 1+eps] so the reader sees the drift starting to clip. Caption tag: 'step 0, policy has NOT moved -> ratio SHOULD be exactly 1.0, but numerics make it drift. Clip engages on phantom off-policyness. Silent bug.'

PANEL B -- 'The fix (recompute old logprobs on the trainer)'. Same token stream, but now BOTH the numerator and the denominator are emitted by the ONE trainer pipeline (a no_grad forward for old_lp, a grad forward for new_lp) -- draw a single shared pipeline box feeding both. The sampler pipeline is demoted to a small greyed side-box tagged 'sampler logprobs -> diagnostics / true off-policy term only'. The ratio track now sits flat and pinned on the 1.0 line across all token positions (clip fraction ~ 0). Caption tag: 'numerator and denominator share the SAME kernels + dtype -> r = exp(new_lp - old_lp) = 1.0 exactly at step 0.'

Bottom one-liner (the intuition): 'It is not the POLICIES that differ -- it is the ARITHMETIC. Make the ratio's top and bottom come from the same pipeline.' Constraints: theme-safe (use accent for 'trainer/correct path', a warn hue only for the drifting/bug ratio and the greyed diagnostic box, muted for supporting text); no hardcoded primaries; invent NO benchmark numbers -- the ratio wobble is purely schematic; everything inside the viewBox with a clear left-to-right, panel-A-then-panel-B reading order; fully understandable as a static frame.

new_figures.name: gtloop-decode-memory-bound
new_figures.concept: The mechanism behind 'generation dominates the clock': one decode step streams the entire weight set from HBM to emit just one token per sequence (tiny arithmetic intensity, memory-bound), whereas training reuses each weight across the whole batch (high intensity, compute-bound).
new_figures.rationale: This is the chapter's headline ('the central quantitative fact of RL infra') and the deepest 'why.' The existing phase-cost-ranking figure states the CONCLUSION (the ranking) and asserts the reason in a text inset, but it never SHOWS the mechanism -- that a full ~14 GB weight read buys only one token per sequence per step, repeated L_g times. Visualizing bytes-moved vs useful-work-done builds the arithmetic-intensity intuition far more durably than a sentence, and it directly motivates every downstream lever (bigger batch amortizes the weight read, continuous batching, async). Priority 2 because it reinforces rather than fills a total gap; it is scoped to the RL framing so it complements, not duplicates, the ranking figure.
new_figures.brief:
A side-by-side contrast of ONE decode step vs ONE training fwd+bwd, built to make arithmetic intensity (useful FLOPs per byte read from HBM) visceral. Shared visual vocabulary: an 'HBM' slab holding a big block labeled 'all N weights (~14 GB for a 7B model in bf16)', a memory-bus arrow, and a compute unit; token tiles come out the far side.

LEFT -- 'DECODE (one step): memory-bandwidth-bound'. Draw the ENTIRE weight block streaming across the bus (thick, saturated arrow = ~N * b_param bytes moved) into the compute unit, and out pops just ONE token per sequence -- show a batch of B little token tiles, all produced from that SINGLE weight read (annotate 'the batch amortizes ONE weight read -> bigger batch is the only real lever'). Add a small 'x L_g steps' loop badge on the whole left side to convey it repeats once per generated token. Intensity meter: a tiny bar labeled 'useful FLOPs per byte read = tiny' -> tags 'arithmetic intensity ~ 0', 'MFU collapses to single-digit %', 'clock set by HBM bandwidth, not FLOPs'.

RIGHT -- 'TRAINING fwd+bwd (one step): compute-bound'. Same weight block, same one weight read from HBM, but now show each weight being REUSED across all B x L_g tokens of the batch -- draw the weight fanning out into a dense grid of many token-position computations (heavy compute, thin memory arrow). Intensity meter: a tall bar 'useful FLOPs per byte read = huge' -> tags 'high arithmetic intensity', '40-55% MFU', 'clock set by compute'.

CENTER punchline strip tying them together: 'Same weight-read tax on both sides -- but decode extracts ONE token per read while training extracts a whole batch's gradient. That is why the FLOP-CHEAPER phase (2N vs 6N) is the wall-clock-DOMINANT one.' Optionally echo the FLOP formulas C_gen ~ 2N * B * L_g (forward only) and C_train ~ 6N * B * L_g * E as small labels, but keep the star of the figure the bytes-vs-useful-work contrast, not the algebra.

Constraints: theme-safe, no hardcoded primary colors (use accent for the memory/bandwidth-limited decode side, a neutral/secondary for the compute-bound training side, muted for annotations); the only numbers allowed are the illustrative '7B / ~14 GB / bf16 / 2 bytes' already used verbatim in the chapter's worked example and the symbolic N, B, L_g, E -- invent NO throughput or timing benchmark numbers; the intensity meters are schematic (relative bar heights, no axis values). Everything inside the viewBox, clear left(decode)-vs-right(train) reading order, fully legible as a static frame in both light and dark themes.

summary: The chapter is figure-rich and well-served: all four existing figures (staleness dial, five-phase anatomy, phase-cost ranking, async-ladder timelines) render correctly and clearly in both themes and each nails a distinct core intuition, so all four are KEEP with only cosmetic nits. The audit surfaces two under-served foundational intuitions. Priority 1 (must-have): a numerics-consistency figure for the importance-ratio-at-step-0 bug -- the chapter's self-described 'single subtlest correctness issue' and 'favorite source of silent RL bugs', which currently has a full warning box but no visual; a before/after diagram makes the fix ('share the arithmetic pipeline, not just the policy') click. Priority 2: a decode-memory-bound mechanism figure that SHOWS why generation dominates the clock (a full weight read from HBM buys only one token per sequence -> tiny arithmetic intensity), visualizing the mechanism that the existing ranking figure only asserts in text. Both are static; a single frame suffices for each.
